schools.summary: Gill: they took the opportunity to expostulate when they brought him the heads of Oreb and Zeeb.
Henry: no sooner was the enemy subdued than they quarrelled.

themes: Why hast thou served us thus, that thou calledst us not... And they did chide with him sharply
pride and envy breeding strife among brethren in the hour of triumph
only by pride cometh contention (Prov. 13:10)

schools.summary: Gill: he answered in a very mild and gentle manner, giving soft words which turn away wrath.
Keil & Delitzsch: Is not the gleaning of Ephraim better than the vintage of Abiezer?

themes: What have I done now in comparison of you? Is not the gleaning of the grapes of Ephraim better than the vintage of Abiezer?
a humble, soft answer that seeks peace rather than honour
in lowliness of mind let each esteem other better than themselves (Phil. 2:3)

schools.summary: Gill: the high honour conferred on them, with which they might be well contented.
Clarke: a soft answer turneth away wrath, their anger abated.

themes: God hath delivered into your hands the princes of Midian... Then their anger was abated toward him
the power of meekness and a soft answer to quell strife
a soft answer turneth away wrath: but grievous words stir up anger (Prov. 15:1)

schools.summary: Gill: neither in the battle nor in the pursuit had he lost one man; they were faint, yet pursuing.

themes: Gideon came to Jordan, and passed over, he, and the three hundred men that were with him, faint, yet pursuing them
the noble constancy that endures to the end despite weariness
let us not be weary in well doing... if we faint not (Gal. 6:9)

schools.summary: Gill: Succoth lay in his way, a city beyond Jordan in the tribe of Gad; he asked bread for the people who followed him, being faint.

themes: Give, I pray you, loaves of bread unto the people that follow me; for they be faint
the people of God owing help to those who fight in the common cause
if thine enemy hunger, feed him (Rom. 12:20)

schools.summary: Gill: the chief magistrates made answer, one in the name of the rest.
Clarke: they feared the Midianites might yet prevail and take vengeance.

themes: Are the hands of Zebah and Zalmunna now in thine hand, that we should give bread unto thine army?
the baseness of withholding help from God's cause in fear and unbelief
the fearful, and unbelieving... shall have their part in the lake (Rev. 21:8)

schools.summary: Gill: he answered the princes of Succoth.
Keil & Delitzsch: when the LORD hath delivered them, I will tear your flesh with thorns.

themes: when the LORD hath delivered Zebah and Zalmunna into mine hand, then I will tear your flesh with the thorns
the faith sure of victory set against the fear that will not venture
whoso... seeth his brother have need, and shutteth up his bowels of compassion (1 John 3:17)

schools.summary: Gill: Penuel, named by Jacob from the Lord's appearing to him there face to face, but here was nothing of God now.
Keil & Delitzsch: they answered him as Succoth had.

themes: he went up thence to Penuel... and the men of Penuel answered him as the men of Succoth had answered him
the place where Jacob saw God face to face now sunk in unbelief
the memory of past mercies not keeping men from present sin

schools.summary: Gill: he spake in a threatening way, sure of having conquered all his enemies and restored peace, of which he had no doubt.
Keil & Delitzsch: the threat against the tower.

themes: When I come again in peace, I will break down this tower
the boldness of faith counting the promised victory as good as done
being fully persuaded that, what he had promised, he was able also to perform (Rom. 4:21)

schools.summary: Gill: the place Karkor, where the kings were with their remaining host.
Clarke: the great number that had fallen.

themes: fifteen thousand men, all that were left... for there fell an hundred and twenty thousand men that drew sword
the staggering completeness of God's victory through few
there is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few (1 Sam. 14:6)

schools.summary: Gill: he went a roundabout way, by those who dwelt in tents, the Arabians; smote the host, for the host was secure.

themes: Gideon went up... and smote the host: for the host was secure
the false security of the wicked broken by sudden judgment
when they shall say, Peace and safety; then sudden destruction cometh (1 Thess. 5:3)

schools.summary: Gill: their host was smitten and thrown into confusion by the sudden approach of Gideon's army; he took the two kings and discomfited all the host.

themes: he... took the two kings of Midian, Zebah and Zalmunna, and discomfited all the host
the LORD's deliverance complete, the princes of the foe brought low
thou hast ascended on high, thou hast led captivity captive (Ps. 68:18)

schools.summary: Gill: he returned to Penuel and Succoth, to chastise them for their ill treatment.
Keil & Delitzsch: him returning victorious from the war.

themes: And Gideon the son of Joash returned from battle before the sun was up
the threatened judgment on those who failed his cause surely to fall
be sure your sin will find you out (Num. 32:23)

schools.summary: Gill: he spied a young man of the city, laid hold on him, and inquired about the chief magistrates; he described the princes and elders, seventy-seven men.

themes: he described unto him the princes of Succoth, and the elders thereof, even threescore and seventeen men
God's judgments exact, falling on the guilty and not at random
the LORD knoweth them that are his (2 Tim. 2:19)

schools.summary: Gill: he entered the city and bespoke the inhabitants.
Ellicott: the addition 'that are weary' enhances the guilt of the elders.

themes: Behold Zebah and Zalmunna, with whom ye did upbraid me, saying, Are the hands... now in thine hand?
the LORD vindicating his cause and shaming the unbelief of doubters
let God be true, but every man a liar (Rom. 3:4)

schools.summary: Gill: he took all of them, especially those most guilty, to be made public examples.
Keil & Delitzsch: him taking the elders and teaching them with thorns.

themes: he took the elders of the city, and thorns of the wilderness and briers, and with them he taught the men of Succoth
God chastening the hard-hearted who refuse mercy to his people
he shall have judgment without mercy, that hath shewed no mercy (James 2:13)

schools.summary: Gill: he beat down the tower as he had threatened, and slew the men of the city.

themes: And he beat down the tower of Penuel, and slew the men of the city
no stronghold availing against the judgment of God
though thou exalt thyself as the eagle... thence will I bring thee down (Obad. 1:4)

schools.summary: Gill: this was after he brought them into the land of Canaan, perhaps to his own city Ophrah.
Keil & Delitzsch: they answered, each one resembled the children of a king.

themes: What manner of men were they whom ye slew at Tabor?... each one resembled the children of a king
the blood of the innocent crying for justice
the LORD... will avenge the blood of his servants (Deut. 32:43)

schools.summary: Gill: they were his brethren by his mother's side.
Keil & Delitzsch: if ye had saved them alive, I would not slay you.

themes: They were my brethren, even the sons of my mother... if ye had saved them alive, I would not slay you
the avenging of innocent blood righteous yet without malice
if it be possible... live peaceably with all men (Rom. 12:18)

schools.summary: Gill: Jether, as near kinsman, was a proper person to avenge the blood, and for the greater mortification of the kings; the youth feared.

themes: he said unto Jether his firstborn, Up, and slay them. But the youth drew not his sword: for he feared
great and weighty tasks not for the untried and timid
I have written unto you, young men, because ye are strong (1 John 2:14)

schools.summary: Gill: they chose rather to die by the hand of so great and valiant a commander than by a youth.
Clarke: their words, as the man is, so is his strength.

themes: Rise thou, and fall upon us... And Gideon... slew them, and took away the ornaments that were on their camels' necks
the proud oppressors brought down in their turn
they that take the sword shall perish with the sword (Matt. 26:52)

schools.summary: Gill: the chief men met and sent a deputation offering him the government.
Henry: the offer of a crown.

themes: Rule thou over us, both thou, and thy son, and thy son's son also: for thou hast delivered us
gratitude tempting to exalt a man into the place that belongs to God
it is better to trust in the LORD than to put confidence in princes (Ps. 118:9)

schools.summary: Gill: he declined the kingship, for which he had no call from God, the choice of a king belonging to him; the LORD shall rule over you.

themes: I will not rule over you, neither shall my son rule over you: the LORD shall rule over you
the noble refusal of self-exaltation, owning God alone as King
the LORD is our king; he will save us (Isa. 33:22)

schools.summary: Gill: he thought they would scarcely deny this, since they had offered him a crown.
Clarke: the request for the earrings.

themes: I would desire a request of you, that ye would give me every man the earrings of his prey
even the best of men may take a wrong step after a right one
let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall (1 Cor. 10:12)

schools.summary: Gill: they gave them with all their hearts, most freely and cheerfully.
Keil & Delitzsch: them spreading a garment and casting in the earrings.

themes: We will willingly give them. And they spread a garment, and did cast therein every man the earrings of his prey
even cheerful giving must be directed to right uses
God loveth a cheerful giver (2 Cor. 9:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the weight, computed at some eight hundred ounces of gold; besides ornaments, collars, and purple raiment of the kings.

themes: the weight of the golden earrings... was a thousand and seven hundred shekels of gold; beside ornaments, and collars, and purple raiment
riches, even given of God, carrying their peril
they that will be rich fall into temptation and a snare (1 Tim. 6:9)

schools.summary: Gill: he made an ephod of some of the gold, set up in his city Ophrah; all Israel went a whoring after it, and it became a snare.

debates: The witnesses leave his motive uncertain — perhaps a memorial of the victory, or a means of inquiring of God; whatever the intent, it was unwarranted, apart from the appointed tabernacle, and became idolatry.
themes: Gideon made an ephod thereof... and all Israel went thither a whoring after it: which thing became a snare
the well-meant device of a good man turned to idolatry and ruin
in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men (Matt. 15:9)

schools.summary: Gill: Midian was humbled and brought under, their power over Israel broken.
Keil & Delitzsch: the country in quietness forty years.

themes: Thus was Midian subdued... they lifted up their heads no more. And the country was in quietness forty years
the complete and lasting peace God gives his people
he maketh wars to cease... he breaketh the bow (Ps. 46:9)

schools.summary: Gill: the war being ended, he disbanded his army and retired to his own house at Ophrah.

themes: And Jerubbaal the son of Joash went and dwelt in his own house
the great deliverer content with a private station, free from ambition
study to be quiet, and to do your own business (1 Thess. 4:11)

schools.summary: Gill: this was the number of all his sons; he had children before, as Jether his firstborn; he had many wives.

themes: And Gideon had threescore and ten sons of his body begotten: for he had many wives
even the faithful may follow the world's ways to their hurt
neither shall he multiply wives to himself, that his heart turn not away (Deut. 17:17)

schools.summary: Gill: she was not a harlot, but a secondary wife, of the meaner sort; the son was named Abimelech.

themes: his concubine that was in Shechem, she also bare him a son, whose name he called Abimelech
the seed of future trouble sown in an irregular union
they have sown the wind, and they shall reap the whirlwind (Hos. 8:7)

schools.summary: Gill: he lived some forty years after the war, blessed with a large family, wealth, esteem, and favour with God and men; buried in the sepulchre of his father.

themes: And Gideon the son of Joash died in a good old age, and was buried in the sepulchre of Joash his father
God honouring his faithful servant with a peaceful, honoured end
thou shalt come to thy grave in a full age, like as a shock of corn (Job 5:26)

schools.summary: Gill: they turned from God and the pure worship of him to idolatry.
Keil & Delitzsch: them making Baal-berith their god.

themes: as soon as Gideon was dead, that the children of Israel turned again, and went a whoring after Baalim, and made Baalberith their god
reformation held only by the leader, failing when he is gone
there arose another generation... which knew not the LORD (Judg. 2:10)

schools.summary: Gill: they forgot and forsook him, which showed base ingratitude; he had delivered them from all their enemies on every side.

themes: the children of Israel remembered not the LORD their God, who had delivered them out of the hands of all their enemies
to forget the LORD and his benefits the beginning of apostasy
bless the LORD, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits (Ps. 103:2)

schools.summary: Gill: they showed great unkindness and cruelty, slaying his seventy sons, as related in the next chapter.
Keil & Delitzsch: their failure to show kindness to his house.

themes: Neither shewed they kindness to the house of Jerubbaal, namely, Gideon, according to all the goodness which he had shewed
forsaking God hardening the heart against all goodness
men shall be... unthankful, unholy, without natural affection (2 Tim. 3:2-3)
